speaker: interviewer
text: --how you found your relative in Wisconsin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, OK. So we did-- starting from Appleton, I think that part, he has it, where I went there. And so from Appleton we did come to Milwaukee. And he brought me with some other refugees.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was talking to them. And they asked me from what town I am. And I told them Miedzyrzecz. And I says, what are the people here-- out here? He says, well, they're some from Brezhnev, and their name is Reicht. And at that time, I-- I didn't-- he said, well, Reicht. I says, well, that is my mother's last name. And he says, why don't I call and find out? He said to me, you know, I'm going to tell you, the old man is dead.

speaker: interviewee
text: But his wife is alive. And she's a very nice woman. So then we called the-- the number once, and it's busy. In the meantime he said, well, we're going to call once more. Otherwise we-- we take off, we go for home. So now he says, OK, let's try. And we call in there. And then my aunt, you know, she picked up the telephone. And I-- and she asked me where I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you know, she-- it's like in Europe, you find out what was your mother's name? What was your father's name? What were they doing? Where is your mother coming from? Blah, blah, blah. So they know right away who you are and you are not. So they say, you know, your relative are going to come and get you. And he says OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I didn't stay there but six weeks, about two months, till I got organized. And then I went on my own. But they were very cordial. And when I got married they were-- they were very nice though.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Jewish Center. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There were some women. I don't know, I just picked that woman. [LAUGHS] Well, and she was in Jewish-- she was a Jewish single or something, in Jewish Center. And I was there. And-- and she was there. And before you know, I took her out, and it clicked. And that's all I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was not from a survivor background?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. She's American. She lived in, then, in a small town. She probably-- more antisemitism than-- than-- than I did in Poland. Because she was right there, only two families in the little town. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a hard adjustment to make, to have a-- a wife who didn't speak your language, who didn't have your background?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My wife, you know, she-- she worked all them years. And she helped support our family, as I did. So-- and there's not too many women willing to do that. She had to get up early in the morning and she is a good wife. I can't complain. She has to get up at-- I mean, not she has to. But she does get up every morning and get me breakfast and send me off to work. You know. Well, you know. And then with two little children, and we don't have nobody, what-- we had to raise the children by ourselves. I mean, I didn't have no relatives to help us. So it was-- first couple years, it was kind of rough. But we wants to look back on all of them other thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you grew up, before the war, in a religious family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you were sitting there in the forest--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you think-- what did you think about God and religion--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah--

speaker: interviewer
text: What had happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And most of them in there says, forget about all of that. That's-- and if there is something there, it wouldn't let them kill that many innocent people for no reason at all. Why would God-- if he's-- as any, you know, why don't you say it? [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Any power?

speaker: interviewee
text: Any power. Why would they let do it? I mean, I can-- we can see that the older people may be seeing or what the younger people, their children. They didn't know nothing about it. They didn't hurt nobody. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Most of them were atheist. Or an atheist, excuse me. But then as the years go by, you just maybe mellow and think a little different.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you bring your children up with a Jewish background?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They were-- he was bar mitzvahed. He was taken to Sunday school. And I was even a member in this temple all them years. I'm not religious though. I don't go too many times to the temple. But I belong to the temple now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we're coming to a closing. Is there anything that we haven't talked about that we should have talked about? Or any story that comes to your mind that you didn't tell?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, one story I remember. [LAUGHS] I mean, we had a cat. And-- and I don't know, I suppose I was a little devil too. And I see there was some wine. And I figure well, let-- let's see. I'm going to give the cat a little wine and see what he's going to do. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You know what? That cat broke all the windows in the house.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] But I didn't want to tell it to nobody till I come in this country. My brother didn't even know that. And he couldn't tell it to nobody. Well, this is the only secret I hold from everyone. I didn't want to tell it. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you and your brother become reunited?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who?

speaker: interviewer
text: You and your brother, after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, there were groups. And he went. I had him in. He was in Czechoslovakia. And I was talking with people.

speaker: interviewee
text: They tell me yeah, there is-- what is your name? Sternsczs? There's someone named Sternsczs in Czechoslovakia. And they had a couple camps there. You know, what camp? And he it said to me. OK, I call up. And-- and there-- oh, in Czechoslovakia, at the telephone stall, not in Poland. He call up. And the guy said to me who I am? And I told him I'm from Miedzyrzecz and my name is Sternsczs. And he said to me, yeah, you have a brother here. I says, my brother here?

speaker: interviewee
text: And so-- so anyway, we had-- you know, so we made arrangements to meet each other. And after I'm going to get off the train, he's going to wait for me. And so when the train-- I took the train to there, I think. And anyway, I got off the train, and I look for half an hour, an hour. Not there. So I had the telephone number, you know, and I call up. And he said, he is on the station there, waiting for you. I says, I'm waiting for half an hour and I can't find-- you know, all the time, and we changed so much. I couldn't find him.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then he says, wait a-- he said to me, wait in the station. I'm coming. And he come and he said-- see? But I didn't see him all through the war. So you-- you see? He-- he went away earlier, 1941, '41 to-- '40-- you know, like four or five years, we didn't see him. At that time, you change a lot. Well-- Had you been close before the war? Or was he too much older than you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He is four years old. He had his friends. And I, with my friends. You know what I mean? Some people-- he-- I didn't bother with his friends. He didn't bother with my friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: But then after the war, when--

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- do you think we got any closer? No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, that was going to be my question. Because you were all that he had, and he was all that you had.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know, it's not working like that. I don't know why. It's right, but I mean, there's certain things in life don't always work out the way you want. And that's one of them things.

speaker: interviewer
text: We talk to each other, we had that. But I mean, certain things, when we make something and we want to-- we-- my wife-- anyway, like my son, he donated money for the-- what do you call it? The temple.

speaker: interviewee
text: From Czechoslovakia, they had then-- you had a special section in the-- and they brought us some-- you know, they bought him some Torahs. And he made a nice donation to the temple that we go. And everybody's going to be there when they're unveiling the Torahs. And his name-- he named-- he-- he put up money for his name, too. And it comes by the end, he didn't show up. Little things like that, that kind of hurts you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any final words that you have--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to leave for your family, for your grandchildren, for the coming generations about all that you went through?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I don't know what to say. [LAUGHS] I'm speechless. And whatever it was, it was. I-- I'm in good hands now. And-- [SIGHS] [CRYING] And my life is good now, and I can't-- I don't have no complaint. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know what to say now. On that note then, we'll end.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I know-- I know my children and my wife are very nice to me. And I-- I can't complain. I have a good life. Which is true, in-- in two words.

speaker: interviewer
text: The New Year is coming, Rosh Hashanah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we wish you the best-- the best on the New Year.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I wish you all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And thank you on behalf of Steven Spielberg--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And survivors of the Shoah Visual History Foundation.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I seen the Shoah-- I seen the Shoah film. But a lot of people can't watch that. I kind of-- then it bothered, but who wouldn't-- wouldn't-- wouldn't it bother? You got to be made-- if you were going to say you see a film like that, and it doesn't bother you, you're not human.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you for sharing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Why can't those other people-- thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you for sharing your story with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Fellas, Jonah, where is your other friend? Where is your other friend?

speaker: interviewer
text: Shirley.

speaker: interviewee
text: Shirley? That's my wife's second name, Shirley. OK, this is my mother. Rif--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rifke, but it's Reicht.

speaker: interviewer
text: Reicht?

speaker: interviewee
text: Reicht. And this is the picture taken in Rowne, in 1935. And the-- the-- what she is having there is the guy was snapshot, walking. And she's getting money in the purse to pay that person.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you happen to have this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This pictures is-- my brother had it. And he gived it to me. This is my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is she on the right or the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: This, on the right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sosie. But Yakov. And on the left is a friend of hers. Her name, unknown. And this was done, I think she was about nine years old--

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: --in Miedzyrzecz, this was done in Miedzyrzecz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what year, about, was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nine years old, what-- what year is that? In 19-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --1931? I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my brother, Yitzchok, then Yakov. And this he took, was 1937, in Miedzyrzecz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this picture also, he gave to you after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: He give it to me. And this picture, he also give it to me. This is the picture from the kibbutz. And I was in Germany, 1945. Til the end of '45, I guess.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm the first guy from the top to the left. And my name is Simha, then Yakov. My name too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, better say it, what it's-- it don't matter. My name is Sy Sterns. And this certificate, it was in Germany, in Regensburg. And it was a course we had to take for three weeks. And I passed it. And I was DP police, well, in Bad Reichenhall. This is me, in-- Sy Sterns, as a police in Bad Reichenhall. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you have this job? From '46 until when?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think I was there, '47. '46, yeah well, the end of '46, '47. All right, this is Sy Sterns. And this is my pass, what I have to-- for riding in the train between Bad Reichenhall, wherever I could go for-- to Munich, I guess. You know, Bad Reichenhall led to Munich. That's it. Well, this is my wife, Joan Sterns. And I am Sy Sterns. And this is the picture taken in 1957.

speaker: interviewer
text: A year after you got married, yeah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We got married in '56. And this is my twins when they were eight years old. And my son is Jay Barry Sterns. And Sheila Renee Sterns. And they were eight years old at the time the picture was taken.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is my wife, Joan S. Sterns, and my daughter, Sheila Renee. OK? That's it. When is it taken, the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken in 1992.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the 40 wedding anniversary. We took a picture. And that was in September-- no?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, September 10th, 1996. From the left is my wife, Joan S. Sterns. And then is my granddaughter, Ellie Sterns. And then Josh. Where is little Josh here?

speaker: interviewer
text: He must be the one with his back to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. And Josh Sterns. And then in the middle is my daughter, Sheila Renee.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is she holding?

speaker: interviewee
text: She's holding Brittany.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's her daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: The man with the microphone?

speaker: interviewee
text: The man with the microphone? [INAUDIBLE]. Oh, Jay. That's my son. Jay [? Barry ?] Sterns. And-- and that's my daughter-in-law, [? Mindy ?] [? Beth ?] Sterns. And that's me, Sy Sterns. From the right is my brother, [? Irving ?] Stokowski. And to the left is Sy Sterns. See, he didn't change his name when--

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in-- I think at the part-- no, no, no, no, no. This picture was taken in '80-- six years ago. That's 1990.

speaker: interviewer
text: Great.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, this is my family. From the top is [? Mindy ?] and Jay. And my grandchildren is Ellie and Josh, and Joan and Sy. [SPEAKING HEBREW] JOAN (WIFE): I've been married to Sy-- it's going to be 40 years this September 30th. And it has been different being married to a survivor. It seems like a lot of your friends are either one or the other, have been survivors. And the children of survivors seem to have a lot in common. Sometimes those are the friends of your children, and that takes-- in your life and their life, sometimes we have felt that he was a little possessive because he lost his whole family. And he-- it's hard for him to let go. And he's-- he's adjusting though. But it took many, many years. But thank God, he's got beautiful grandchildren and children. And it turned out, I think, for him in the United States, as well as it could. Yes. JAY (SON): I'm Jay. I never really realized-- I guess the first time I realized that my father was a Holocaust survivor was around seven or eight years old, when we used to have family holidays. And we did not-- I did have a grandfather that is still alive on my mother's side. But that I never had grandparents on my father's side that I knew. None of my friends, actually growing up, were children of Holocaust survivors. Most of my friends were-- you know-- their parents were born in the United States. Many of their grandparents were born in the United States. But I guess I always felt that we were a little bit different when in Sunday school or Hebrew school, they would ask, you know, talk about the Holocaust. And it was usually I was the only person whose one or both parents were a survivor. So I always felt a little bit different, but different in a positive sense, not a negative sense. A sense of pride, a sense that, in some respects, that you know, we overcame adversity, or my father did. And that maybe we're a little stronger and we have a little more intestinal fortitude. You know, not-- never a quitter. Very strong in terms of ability to persevere through, you know, both good times and obviously bad times as well. And I think those are some of the qualities that you learn by example. MINDY (DAUGHTER-IN-LAW): I've been married to Jay for 10 years. I learned all about the Holocaust in Sunday school and religious school. But I don't think I ever personally really knew anyone who was a survivor--

speaker: interviewer
text: JAY (SON): (WHISPERS) Do you want me to take him?

speaker: interviewee
text: MINDY (DAUGHTER-IN-LAW): --until I entered the Sterns family. And I must say, it was very different from my family growing up. I don't have survivors in my family. You know, now I do. But it's a different experience. And there's difference-- JOSH (GRANDSON): I want to sleep. MINDY (DAUGHTER-IN-LAW): There's differences in--

speaker: interviewer
text: JOAN (WIFE): You want to sit on my lap?

words.text: You want to sit on my

speaker: interviewee
text: MINDY (DAUGHTER-IN-LAW): --I think possessiveness, and just a feeling-- JOSH (GRANDSON): But I want that on. JAY (SON): Shh. MINDY (DAUGHTER-IN-LAW): --of-- JOSH (GRANDSON): I want that on. MINDY (DAUGHTER-IN-LAW): --of different losses. JOSH (GRANDSON): I want that on now. MINDY (DAUGHTER-IN-LAW): And-- JAY (SON): (WHISPERS) Because of the movie.

speaker: interviewer
text: MINDY (DAUGHTER-IN-LAW): Do you know what the word is? It was just different than my family. It was-- it's a wonderful family to be a part of. I am so lucky.

speaker: interviewee
text: JOSH (GRANDSON): Mommy.

speaker: interviewer
text: MINDY (DAUGHTER-IN-LAW): And we have wonderful-- we have a wonderful family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ellie, do you want to say something? ELLIE (GRANDDAUGHTER): I love my grandma and grandpa.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that's nice. JOAN (WIFE): Oh, thank you, Ellie. Can I get a kiss?

speaker: interviewer
text: MINDY (DAUGHTER-IN-LAW): Want to go give grandpa a kiss? Go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: JOSH (GRANDSON): No. MINDY (DAUGHTER-IN-LAW): Go on. JOAN (WIFE): Oh, leave him be. Yeah, he don't want it. JOAN (WIFE): Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Josh, do you want to say something for your grandpa? What do you want to say. JOSH (GRANDSON): I love grandpa and grandma.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHTER] JOAN (WIFE): He loves grandpa and grandma.

speaker: interviewer
text: MINDY (DAUGHTER-IN-LAW): You love your grandma and grandpa?

words.text: You love your grandma and grandpa?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHTER] Really means a lot. JOAN (WIFE): I think he's tired. ELLIE (GRANDDAUGHTER): He isn't. You know he is-- JOAN (WIFE): I love you. [INAUDIBLE]. Hey.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jay, did your father talk about the Holocaust with you? JOAN (WIFE): Do you want to sit on my lap?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. JAY (SON): Not really about the Holocaust itself and what happened during the war. Things that happened before the war, his family, his brother, his parents. You know, he would tell stories about the house that he lived in and the hardware store that they owned. And he would talk about things before the war, but not actually what happened during the war. I think the first time I-- I may have realized that actually, he went through the Holocaust and had a physical scar is you know, when we were younger and you would take your shirt off in summer and go to the swimming pool. And he had a scar along the side where he had some ribs taken out from a shrapnel wound. That was probably the first time, and I guess I was probably six or seven years old when I-- when I asked what that was from. But he never really talked about the actual experiences and you know, what it was like other than to you know, suggest, you know, that no-- that you can't trust anybody, and that you were always on the run and things of that nature. In his bedroom there's a little plaque, I guess, is-- almost like in a frame. And it has the Hebrew names of his parents and the [INAUDIBLE] sites of when he thought they died. And-- and you know, that was always there. It was always a visible reminder in our house. But that's probably some of-- he never really talked, actually, about the-- the Holocaust itself and kind of his experiences between 1941 and '45. He would talk about after the war, and some of the people he knew, and his friends who now live in Chicago and elsewhere, what it was like, and Bad Reichenhall after the war, but not really during it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Has it colored the way you look at the world? Do you feel that people are not trustable and that you have to be looking over your shoulder? And--

speaker: interviewee
text: JAY (SON): I think it's-- it probably has colored things both positively and negatively. I think in some sense, you have a-- a certain sense of compassion, probably for the street person who's-- and the homeless person. Because you realize that's what your family was like.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you kind of have a sense of a-- you know, kind of ah, but for the grace of God, go I. On the other hand, you have this tendency not to trust kind of large institutions, and to not-- not necessarily trust people in general.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the other hand, you kind of have a certain compassion for those who are less fortunate. So I think it cuts both ways, probably. Skepticism is probably a fair word about kind of how you look at humanity.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you concur? MINDY (DAUGHTER-IN-LAW): Yes. Very much so. Jay is-- has a very large heart to people who are less fortunate than him. You know, Jay, have you and your sister always felt protective toward your father for what he'd been through? Or not?

speaker: interviewee
text: JAY (SON): I think I have. I don't think my sister necessarily was. I think she-- I don't-- you know, I've never really discussed it with her. But I think she kind of tuned it out, basically, and wanted to believe it never happened, and never wanted to deal with it. So I think that's kind of-- ELLIE (GRANDDAUGHTER): I want to [INAUDIBLE] carrier. JAY (SON): I think that's kind of how she handled it.

speaker: interviewer
text: ELLIE (GRANDDAUGHTER): Can you carry me? Sy, do you have a last word--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah

speaker: interviewer
text: --for your tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wish everybody and a happy and a healthy New Year. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the same to you, from Steven Spielberg, and the survivors of the Shoah Visual History Foundation. ELLIE (GRANDDAUGHTER): (WHISPERS) Dad, what is that? And thank you, all of you-- JAY (SON): (WHISPERS) The microphone. --for sharing your time and your story with us. And thank you, Ellie. And thank you, Josh. And thank you, everybody. And thank you, Joan.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. JOAN (WIFE): Thank you. JAY (SON): Thank you. ELLIE (GRANDDAUGHTER): Yeah, thank you.
